speaker: interviewer
text: The date is October the 2nd, 1995. The survivor's name is Mr. Nison Katz. That's K-A-T-Z. I am the interviewer. My name is Richard Bassett, B-A-S-S-E-T-T. We are in Toronto, Canada. And the interview will be held in English. The date is October the 2nd, 1995. The survivor's name is Mr. Nison Katz, K-A-T-Z. I am the interviewer. My name is Richard Bassett, B-A-S-S-E-T-T. We are in Toronto, Canada. And the interview will be held in English. What is your name, please?

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell your last name, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: It is-- it was in [NON-ENGLISH] till the war in 1939. And then they-- the-- when the war started, Poland with Russia-- with Germany, so then they divided Russia with Germany, made a pact. Well, a part from Poland go to Germany; a part from Russia, from Ukraine-- from Poland, goes to Russia side. But it is right-- from Russia side. So, so the Russia took it over.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was her maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was a tailor, as I remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was it, in the-- in the house?

speaker: interviewer
text: So you lived in a house?

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe it for me?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was a duplex. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And it's-- contained-- and a bedroom, a living and dining room. I mean, once-- like, it was a living room and a dining room, like here-- and a kitchen.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a one floor, yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the second part is the same thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: The second floor? Is that what you mean--

speaker: interviewer
text: I see what you mean. OK. And was that considered a, a pretty-- was that a wealthy living, or, or middle class?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, as I remember-- well, I was a kid then. Oh, I think it was a low-middle class.

speaker: interviewer
text: There was no middle class. So you either sort of-- you had money, or you didn't.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we had-- we had money when we had worked-- when we worked. When we didn't work, it was very bad. That's what I remember.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it's-- it was, like, contracting. He used to take out the-- I mean, work from the stores. There was stores. They used to sell the goods in their store. They-- it got to-- here, they got to make up the garments. So they gave him the material. And he used to make it up and give it back to, to, to the stores.

speaker: interviewer
text: So were these gentiles or Jews?

speaker: interviewer
text: He didn't have any non-Jewish customers?

speaker: interviewer
text: So was Ostrog mostly a Jewish town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it about-- was-- where, where, where we lived?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the part was the most Jewish. It was all together about 10,000 Jewish families.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- but there were some non-Jewish families as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, sure. But they, they, they, they lived on the outskirts of, of the city, the-- most of them--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the name of the street you lived on?

speaker: interviewer
text: And tell me, you had brothers or sisters?

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me their names, from the oldest one?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well the eldest one is Shepa, and then Yosef, and then Sosie, and then Brucha.

speaker: interviewee
text: --Sosie and Brucha and Nuson. And then--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Do you know when your brothers and sisters were born--

speaker: interviewer
text: --or at least how many years there were between you?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was apart in years, about two, three, years but not, not more. Well, from, from my sister, before me, it was apart of 10 years, 10 years about.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your oldest brothers and sisters were, were quite a bit older than you, then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, sure. They all were older.

speaker: interviewer
text: But I mean a lot older.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, did any of them survive the war?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did any of your sisters or brothers survive the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents-- my father died in 1927. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Then my brothers took over. I mean, they were tailors, too. I mean, they took up the job of that my father left. They, they been taking out materials from the stores-- and the same things, same procedures my father did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me a bit about your home life? Were you religious? What language did you speak?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, what I remember is from a little one on, I went to cheder. Still about 13, 13-- bar mitzvah. The bar mitzvah-- my bar mitzvah was, what I remember, I mean, I haven't got the father. And it wasn't-- of course, it wasn't bar mitzvahs like we are making it here now. What I remember my bar mitzvah is the rabbi. My rabbi was the-- teach me the-- he didn't have to teach me because I went there every day after school. It was on a Tuesday. And he came over to, to my house. And he took me. And he took me to the mikveh first. After that, he took me to the shul. Because Tuesday, they're reading the Torah. So-- and I had [NON-ENGLISH] at the Torah. And then we went home. On Saturday-- and when we went to shul with the rabbi, I, I mean, I had a little speech. I don't remember. But then from there, from the shul, the family, the immediate family, came to our house. And we have like a l'Chaim. That's what-- my bar mitzvah, what. But being a kid, I remember my father was home. I mean, I, I was 9 years old, 10 years old. But I still don't remember him very well. But I do remember what he did to me, like, on a Friday after-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] excuse me-- 12 o'clock. He used to put me-- it was a table. And it was benches around the table. He used to put me on a bench. And he used to-- I got to tell him the Seder from the week and everything. And then he let me go to play. And that's what I remember, my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the name of the shul that you had your bar mitzvah in and the name of the rabbi?

speaker: interviewee
text: It, it wasn't a rabbi like we're having rabbis here. He was a plain teacher, a rebbe, what-- he hasn't got a congregation, hasn't got anything. That wasn't in at that time, what--

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you know the name of the shul?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I do. I mean, we had a shul. It was like a tailor shul. It's called-- well, the-- most of them used to be tailors. I mean, [NON-ENGLISH] or--

speaker: interviewee
text: --used to go there-- yeah. They used to go there during the week. But on a Saturday and holidays, we had a special minyan. I, I do have-- I'll, I'll show you later on the picture of the-- of the minyan, the rabbi, his name is right in the picture, in the book. I'll show you it later.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, well, I mean, on Saturdays and in the holidays, we used to go there. Because my father was a Chusid of the rabbi. And when he passed away, the gabbai took it over. And the old Hasidim would belong to the congregation before used to go to the-- to the gabbai on Saturdays, only Saturdays and the holidays. That's what I remember. What language did you speak at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, at home, we spoke Yiddish.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the language of Ostrog? Was it Ukrainian or Polish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was, actually is-- belonged to Poland. But it was a lot Ukrainian--

speaker: interviewer
text: So you spoke Polish and Ukrainian as well?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Tell me, who were your friends? Who did you play with?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I had lots of friends in the beginning. But I'll start on Ha-no'ar Ha-Ziyyoni. When I was about 10, 11, or 12 years, I belonged to an organization, Ha-no'ar Ha-Ziyyoni.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there, we had, I mean, like, it was a [NON-ENGLISH], or it was several [NON-ENGLISH], I mean, according to the age or what.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the most I, I been there, it was one the name Katz, too, but not a relative. His name was-- the first name was Friedl-- we used to call him [NON-ENGLISH]-- Katz. Then it was Zalman Szejdenberg what is still-- I mean, he was a captain in Russia. During the war, he was in jail because he was-- he was [NON-ENGLISH] in, in the-- in the Hashomer-- Ha-no'ar Ha-Ziyyoni. And then it was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Muni Smik. I met him in, in Russia after the war. And then it was Joski Mela. He is still in, in Israel. This was the most-- and on the street where we lived, I had some more kids who used to play, like, a Katz again, Mojshe Katz. We used to go in the same-- in the same class and-- doing a lecture, Polish or whatever. But-- like boys in this age, well, we didn't set-- we didn't sit still. You know how it is. So when the teacher used to come into the classroom, I used to say, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. This means, one Katz stays on the knees. The other one goes out. And the children remains in the class. What-- so that was, I mean-- and when I went to cheder, there, I had other kids what I used to-- I used to play with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you only played with-- you only had Jewish friends?

speaker: interviewer
text: And you didn't have any non-Jewish friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. There wasn't non-Jewish because the most, it was the--

speaker: interviewee
text: --Jewish neighborhood. In school, what, I mean, it was a mix. Like, it was a public school. It was-- there are Polish, Russian, Ukrainian, all kinds. But there, they used to give us a terrible-- they used to call us 'Zyd' and all kinds of names.

speaker: interviewee
text: The teachers, I, I don't remember. One teacher, I do remember what-- I mean, he was a teacher of music. And when he used to come into the school, he used to say, like, I mean, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 'Chaim,' or 'Moishe'-- we used to-- Jewish pronounce 'chhha.' He used to, like, he used to call on somebody, 'Chhhaim.' He used to pronounce the 'chhha'-- what. He gave you a little bit--

speaker: interviewee
text: And another teacher I remember-- it was a math teacher. But he was a [NON-ENGLISH]. He was-- I think he was a German guy, what. But he knew Hebrew. But he used to come into the school. And, and at the lecture, he used to write down on the table, I mean, on the board, some Hebrew words. But actually, this writing, it just gave you needles, what. That's what I remember in, in school.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I do not. I never--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my father's-- on the father's side--

speaker: interviewee
text: --it was Shejman. Because-- I know because when I, I say [NON-ENGLISH], so I remind him. And the mother's name, the grandmother, I don't know. I just know-- and on the mother's side, so I'm after my grandfather, Nison. He was called Nison. But grandmother, I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your father and mother have brothers or sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother had a sister, as I remember, in Rovno. This was another city, 30 kilometers from our city. She had a, a sister there, what, as I remember. And she had a daughter. They used to come to us once in a while.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the sister's name, the family name?

speaker: interviewer
text: And how about on your father's side?

speaker: interviewee
text: On my father's side, I do remember he had a stepbrother. I do remember-- he had.

speaker: interviewee
text: And his name was-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I knew-- I, I, I can't--

speaker: interviewee
text: But I can't recall it. But I, I know he was-- we were in Poland. He was in Russia, on the Russian side there, all the years. And we met them after Russia-- I mean, they took over Poland. So then, the border was opened.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we could go to each other's house since then. I, I knew that he has a stepbrother What-- he has-- I've been there. Like, he had two sons. One, Shejman, and one [NON-ENGLISH]. The stepbrother?

speaker: interviewer
text: --I mean the father, yeah, had, had two--

speaker: interviewee
text: And, and, and, and, and one-- three sons. And one, [NON-ENGLISH]-- this I remember-- [NON-ENGLISH], he was the oldest. And so when Russia took over Poland, and the border opened, so I, I start to have contact with them. And they told me that they have a brother, [NON-ENGLISH], he is still in the army, in the Russian Army during the war-- he was, what.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I-- we, we write to each other letters. They gave me their address. So we had-- I had contact with, with the older son. And the point was, he was killed at the end of war.

speaker: interviewee
text: But the, the other two brothers-- one [NON-ENGLISH], one Shejman-- [NON-ENGLISH] is now in Israel. And Shejman, I don't know. I guess he was in Israel, too. And of-- and they had-- he had a daughter. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Her name-- she is now in Israel, too. I forgot the name. I forgot the name. But she is in Israel. We had contact with her in Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Katz, do you know how far back your family goes in Ostrog? Were your parents born there? Were your grandparents born there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't recall. I know my father wasn't born in Ostrog. He was born in Constantinople, in Russia, I guess, it was. What-- and I, I don't know the whole story. During the war, they emigrated to Ostrog, or something like that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. But they-- he, he wasn't born in Ostrog.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm. Do you know why he emigrated?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I do not. I don't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, actually, I-- it wasn't any discussions. I was a kid. And usually at that time, it wasn't the discussions like we're having it now here. It was altogether a bit different. What, everybody was busy to, to, to make a living if they could, what. They haven't got time for the kids.

speaker: interviewer
text: And there, of course, we didn't have so many questions. I mean, like, the father says, this is it. You haven't got any say on it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] What? So I really don't know. What was the situation? You told me that you were a little boy when your father passed away. So how did your mother manage?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, like I told you before, the brothers took over--

speaker: interviewee
text: --the factory. The factory-- I mean, the shop.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they worked. I've went to school. I still went to school there in a public school. Coming home from school, when I was younger, I used to-- I used to go in cheder for the whole day. Coming in the evenings, I used to have to make homework to school, to the public school. And, and that-- and that-- and that's all what it was, what.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were any of the older kids at home still?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, sure. Nobody was, except Shepa was married. But at that I time, nobody was married-- when my father passed away. Nobody was married then. So everybody was at home, or what. When he passed away, I mean, they took over. And they run the business, or what. And then, when they got married, all of them, but I left-- the only one. So, well, I mean, I, I finished public school. I went to cheder after 13 years. I mean, I went here in yeshiva. And then I start to work in the business, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when all the brothers and sisters married-- so I left with one brother, Nuson, the youngest-- the oldest from me. What-- so we, we used to work together.

speaker: interviewee
text: When he got married, I took over. I took over by myself. I was then maybe, I don't know, 18 years old or-- and so-- and I got to support my mother my sister. My mother was old. My sister wasn't well. And so I took it over. And so-- and I support the whole family till 1939.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember your sisters' husbands and your-- and your-- and your brothers' wives's names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my sister, Sosie's husband was Mayer Magjenborm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Magjenborm, yeah. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My brother's wife was Erna.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was Erna. My brother Nuson's wife was Kela. And my sister's-- the other sister's husband, was Leib Wenokur. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That's all what it is.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my brother, Shepa, he had [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] two boys and two girls. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Well, two started to work. The oldest son, my brother's-- the oldest son, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he went away the same as I did. But I didn't know where and how. He left-- of course, he was in Ostrog-- left there and got killed. The younger son, by chance, I met him in the woods. That's my nephew. I met him in the woods. And from then on, we went together. We've been together till they took me in the army from there. Afterwards, he-- they took him in the army too, afterwards. And he got killed in the army. But it-- it's, I mean, it's caused before that. What-- when we went during the war, I mean, in 1939, the 21st May-- this was '41, in 1941, when the war started with, with Germany. That's when we start to-- we start to run. And so-- but like I said, I met my nephew in, in the woods. And we've been together. And since then, we start to, to work. We worked in one city. The Germans is behind us, or what. We, we, we stayed overnight, sometimes not overnight. And we start-- and we walked till we got into Shepetivka. In Shepetivka, that was [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] a central point from the-- from the trains, or what. So we took a train from there. And I didn't care where I'm going.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I didn't care where I'm going--

speaker: interviewee
text: --just, just to go. And it was two trains. And, I mean, people, I mean, said one to the other, I mean, this train goes to Dnepropetrovsk. This goes to Kiev, what.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I sit down in a train. And-- a train, it was a freight train, a open-- or what. And I sit down there. And I'm waiting. I mean, sometimes we used to wait, four, five, sometimes a day, and two days, on the-- on the train.

speaker: interviewee
text: And during that time, you got to eat. Nobody was feeding us. And we run out. And I don't know. It was-- still was Russian there. There was stores open-- some stores was open. And to get a piece of bread, or what, you got to stay there in the line maybe for half a day. Sometimes we stood there for half a day. And when it comes to the end, we didn't get it. It's run out, or what.

speaker: interviewer
text: So while I was sitting there at the train with my nephew, someone, a former girlfriend, and, and now, the future wife, or what, she went with her parents. So we didn't know from each other. They left without me. I left without them.

speaker: interviewee
text: But this, by chance, or what-- they had to go on another-- to another train. And they got into our train. So we met there, or what. And from there, we went, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] like-- we went in Shepetivka. And the, the Germans start to bombard the, the city. So we, we are-- and, and the train left already. So we, we went to Kiev.
